The greatest honor for even the most important leaders is to be servants of Christ. And those who are not truly saints on earth will never be saints in heaven. Outside of Christ, even the best saints are sinners and unable to stand before God. There is no peace without grace. Inner peace comes from a sense of God’s favor. And there is no grace or peace except from God our Father, the source and origin of all blessings. At Philippi, the apostle was treated badly and saw little result from his work; yet he remembers Philippi with joy. We must thank God for the graces and comforts, gifts and usefulness of others, as we receive the benefit and God receives the glory. The work of grace will not be perfected until the day of Jesus Christ, the day of his appearance. But we can always be confident that God will perform his good work in every soul where he has truly begun it by regeneration; though we must not trust in outward appearances or anything but a new creation to holiness. People are dear to their ministers when they benefit from their ministry. Fellow-sufferers in the cause of God should be dear to one another.

Should we not pity and love those souls whom Christ loves and pities? Those who have any grace need to have more. Try different things so that we may approve what is excellent. The truths and laws of Christ are excellent, and they recommend themselves as such to any thoughtful mind. Sincerity is what we should demonstrate in our interactions with the world, and it is the glory of all our graces. Christians should not be easily offended, and should be very careful not to offend God or their fellow believers. The things that most honor God will most benefit us. Let us not leave it uncertain whether any good fruit is found in us. A small measure of Christian love, knowledge, and fruitfulness should not satisfy anyone.

The apostle was a prisoner at Rome; and to lessen the offense caused by the cross, he shows the wisdom and goodness of God in his sufferings. These things made him known where he otherwise would never have been known, and led some to inquire about the gospel. He suffered from false friends as well as enemies. How wretched is the attitude of those who preached Christ out of envy and argument, and to add affliction to the bonds that oppressed this best of men! The apostle was at peace amidst all things. Since our troubles may lead to good for many, we ought to rejoice. Whatever leads to our salvation comes by the Spirit of Christ; and prayer is the appointed way of seeking it. Our expectation and hope should not be to be honored by people or to escape suffering, but to be upheld in temptation, contempt, and affliction. Let us leave it to Christ how he will make us useful for his glory, whether by labor or suffering, by diligence or patience, by living to his honor in working for him, or dying to his honor in suffering for him.

Death is a great loss to a worldly person, because they lose all their earthly comforts and hopes; but to a true believer it is gain, because it ends all weakness and misery. It delivers them from all the evils of life and brings them into possession of the greatest good. The apostle’s difficulty was not between living in this world and living in heaven; there is no comparison between those two; but between serving Christ in this world and enjoying him in another. Not between two bad things, but between two good things: living for Christ and being with him. See the power of faith and God’s grace; it can make us willing to die. In this world we are surrounded by sin; but when we are with Christ, we will escape sin and temptation, sorrow and death forever. But those who have the most reason to want to depart should be willing to remain in the world as long as God has work for them to do. And the more unexpected blessings are before they arrive, the more of God will be seen in them.

Those who profess the gospel of Christ should live as it suits those who believe its truths, submit to its laws, and depend on its promises. The original word “conversation” denotes the conduct of citizens who seek the credit, safety, peace, and prosperity of their city. There is something worth striving for in the faith of the gospel; there is opposition, and there is a need to strive. A person may sleep and go to hell; but whoever wants to go to heaven must look around and be diligent. There can be unity of heart and affection among Christians even when they disagree about many things. Faith is God’s gift on behalf of Christ; the ability and desire to believe are from God. And if we suffer reproach and loss for Christ, we should consider them gifts and value them accordingly. Yet salvation must not be attributed to bodily afflictions, as though afflictions and worldly persecution deserved it; but salvation comes only from God: faith and patience are his gifts.
